[[Category:kk]] [[Category:Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)]][[en:Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[sn:Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)]][[sn:Takarazuka Kofun (Mie)]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Group of Kofun period Kofuns in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takarazuka Kofun | native_name = 美旗古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Funagata-haniwa 01.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Boat-shaped ''haniwa'' from Takarazuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Takarazuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|00|N|136|30|55|E|region:JP_type:edu|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsusaka, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = [[Kofun]] cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takarazuka Kofun'''|宝塚古墳||}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Takarazuka neighborhood of the city of [[Matsusaka, Mie|Matsusaka]], [[Mie Prefecture]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190992|title=宝塚古墳|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Takarazuka Kofun are located on low hills on the right bank of the Sakanai River, approximately three kilometers to the south of the center of the city of Matsusaka. The site was first [[Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] in 1928 by a team from [[Mie University]] under the direction of Dr Toshio Sato, and was found to be part of a larger cluster of at least 88 ''kofun'', of which 26 were relatively intact. The site was named the {{nihongo|'''Hanaoka Kofun Group'''|花岡古墳群|}} and was proclaimed a National Historic Site in 1932. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)-1, kouenbu.jpg|Takarazuka Kofun No.1 File:Takaraduka-2go-fun 01.JPG|Takarazuka Kofun No.2 File:宝塚1号墳出土 家形埴輪-1.JPG|House-shaped ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 盾形埴輪 (主墳丘出土).JPG|Shield-shaped ''haniwa'' </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)-1, kouenbu.jpg|Takarazuka Kofun No.1 File:Takaraduka-2go-fun 01.JPG|Takarazuka Kofun No.2 File:宝塚1号墳出土 家形埴輪-1.JPG|House-shaped ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 盾形埴輪 (主墳丘出土).JPG|Shield-shaped ''haniwa'' </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

{{Short description|Group of Kofun period Kofuns in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takarazuka Kofun | native_name = 美旗古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Funagata-haniwa 01.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Boat-shaped ''haniwa'' from Takarazuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Takarazuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|00|N|136|30|55|E|region:JP_type:edu|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsusaka, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = [[Kofun]] cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takarazuka Kofun'''|宝塚古墳||}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Takarazuka neighborhood of the city of [[Matsusaka, Mie|Matsusaka]], [[Mie Prefecture]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190992|title=宝塚古墳|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Takarazuka Kofun are located on low hills on the right bank of the Sakanai River, approximately three kilometers to the south of the center of the city of Matsusaka. The site was first [[Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] in 1928 by a team from [[Mie University]] under the direction of Dr Toshio Sato, and was found to be part of a larger cluster of at least 88 ''kofun'', of which 26 were relatively intact. The site was named the {{nihongo|'''Hanaoka Kofun Group'''|花岡古墳群|}} and was proclaimed a National Historic Site in 1932. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)-1, kouenbu.jpg|Takarazuka Kofun No.1 File:Takaraduka-2go-fun 01.JPG|Takarazuka Kofun No.2 File:宝塚1号墳出土 家形埴輪-1.JPG|House-shaped ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 盾形埴輪 (主墳丘出土).JPG|Shield-shaped ''haniwa'' </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]

;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.

{{Short description|Group of Kofun period Kofuns in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takarazuka Kofun | native_name = 美旗古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Funagata-haniwa 01.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Boat-shaped ''haniwa'' from Takarazuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Takarazuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|00|N|136|30|55|E|region:JP_type:edu|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsusaka, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = [[Kofun]] cluster | Group = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takarazuka Kofun'''|宝塚古墳||}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Takarazuka neighborhood of the city of [[Matsusaka, Mie|Matsusaka]], [[Mie Prefecture]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190992|title=宝塚古墳|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Takarazuka Kofun are located on low hills on the right bank of the Sakanai River, approximately three kilometers to the south of the center of the city of Matsusaka. The site was first [[Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] in 1928 by a team from [[Mie University]] under the direction of Dr Toshio Sato, and was found to be part of a larger cluster of at least 88 ''kofun'', of which 26 were relatively intact. The site was named the {{nihongo|'''Hanaoka Kofun Group'''|花岡古墳群|}} and was proclaimed a National Historic Site in 1932. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.

[[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.

{{Short description| part_of of Kofun period Kofuns in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takarazuka Kofun | native_name = 美旗古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Funagata-haniwa 01.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Boat-shaped ''haniwa'' from Takarazuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Takarazuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|00|N|136|30|55|E|region:JP_type:edu|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsusaka, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = [[Kofun]] cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takarazuka Kofun'''|宝塚古墳||}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Takarazuka neighborhood of the city of [[Matsusaka, Mie|Matsusaka]], [[Mie Prefecture]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190992|title=宝塚古墳|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Takarazuka Kofun are located on low hills on the right bank of the Sakanai River, approximately three kilometers to the south of the center of the city of Matsusaka. The site was first [[Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] in 1928 by a team from [[Mie University]] under the direction of Dr Toshio Sato, and was found to be part of a larger cluster of at least 88 ''kofun'', of which 26 were relatively intact. The site was named the {{nihongo|'''Hanaoka Kofun Group'''|花岡古墳群|}} and was proclaimed a National Historic Site in 1932. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.

When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.

{{Short description| part_of of Kofun period Kofuns in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takarazuka Kofun | native_name = 美旗古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Funagata-haniwa 01.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Boat-shaped ''haniwa'' from Takarazuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Takarazuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|00|N|136|30|55|E|region:JP_type:edu|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsusaka, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = [[Kofun]] cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takarazuka Kofun'''|宝塚古墳||}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Takarazuka neighborhood of the city of [[Matsusaka, Mie|Matsusaka]], [[Mie Prefecture]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190992|title=宝塚古墳|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Takarazuka Kofun are located on low hills on the right bank of the Sakanai River, approximately three kilometers to the south of the center of the city of Matsusaka. The site was first [[Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] in 1928 by a team from [[Mie University]] under the direction of Dr Toshio Sato, and was found to be part of a larger cluster of at least 88 ''kofun'', of which 26 were relatively intact. The site was named the {{nihongo|'''Hanaoka Kofun Group'''|花岡古墳群|}} and was proclaimed a National Historic Site in 1932. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)-1, kouenbu.jpg|Takarazuka Kofun No.1 File:Takaraduka-2go-fun 01.JPG|Takarazuka Kofun No.2 File:宝塚1号墳出土 家形埴輪-1.JPG|House-shaped ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 盾形埴輪 (主墳丘出土).JPG|Shield-shaped ''haniwa'' </gallery>

{{Short description| part_of of Kofun period Kofuns in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takarazuka Kofun | native_name = 美旗古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Funagata-haniwa 01.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Boat-shaped ''haniwa'' from Takarazuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Takarazuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|00|N|136|30|55|E|region:JP_type:edu|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsusaka, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = [[Kofun]] cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takarazuka Kofun'''|宝塚古墳||}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Takarazuka neighborhood of the city of [[Matsusaka, Mie|Matsusaka]], [[Mie Prefecture]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)-1, kouenbu.jpg|Takarazuka Kofun No.1 File:Takaraduka-2go-fun 01.JPG|Takarazuka Kofun No.2 File:宝塚1号墳出土 家形埴輪-1.JPG|House-shaped ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 盾形埴輪 (主墳丘出土).JPG|Shield-shaped ''haniwa'' </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)}} *[http://inoues.net/ruins/mie_takaraduka.htm Report on excavations with photos] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.city.matsusaka.mie.jp/www/contents/1337998943646/index.html Matsusaka City Cultural Center home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.city.matsusaka.mie.jp/www/contents/1335845290779/index.html Matsusaka City Hall home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.bunka.pref.mie.lg.jp/bunkazai/da/daItemDetail?mngnum=731022&pageCur=6&pub_id=1 Mie Prefecture Board of Education] {{in lang|ja}}

{{Short description| part_of of Kofun period Kofuns in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takarazuka Kofun | native_name = 美旗古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Funagata-haniwa 01.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Boat-shaped ''haniwa'' from Takarazuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Takarazuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|00|N|136|30|55|E|region:JP_type:edu|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsusaka, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = [[Kofun]] cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takarazuka Kofun'''|宝塚古墳||}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Takarazuka neighborhood of the city of [[Matsusaka, Mie|Matsusaka]], [[Mie Prefecture]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190992|title=宝塚古墳|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Takarazuka Kofun are located on low hills on the right bank of the Sakanai River, approximately three kilometers to the south of the center of the city of Matsusaka. The site was first [[Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] in 1928 by a team from [[Mie University]] under the direction of Dr Toshio Sato, and was found to be part of a larger cluster of at least 88 ''kofun'', of which 26 were relatively intact. The site was named the {{nihongo|'''Hanaoka Kofun Group'''|花岡古墳群|}} and was proclaimed a National Historic Site in 1932. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.

Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.

The site was first [[Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] in 1928 by a team from [[Mie University]] under the direction of Dr Toshio Sato, and was found to be part of a larger cluster of at least 88 ''kofun'', of which 26 were relatively intact. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}

These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)-1, kouenbu.jpg|Takarazuka Kofun No.1 File:Takaraduka-2go-fun 01.JPG|Takarazuka Kofun No.2 File:宝塚1号墳出土 家形埴輪-1.JPG|House-shaped ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 盾形埴輪 (主墳丘出土).JPG|Shield-shaped ''haniwa'' </gallery>

{{Short description| part_of of Kofun period Kofuns in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Takarazuka Kofun | native_name = 美旗古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Funagata-haniwa 01.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Boat-shaped ''haniwa'' from Takarazuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Mie Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Takarazuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|34|33|00|N|136|30|55|E|region:JP_type:edu|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Matsusaka, Mie]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Kansai region]] | type = [[Kofun]] cluster | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no public facilities) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takarazuka Kofun'''|宝塚古墳||}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]]s located in the Takarazuka neighborhood of the city of [[Matsusaka, Mie|Matsusaka]], [[Mie Prefecture]] in the [[Kansai region]] of [[Japan]]. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1932.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/190992|title=宝塚古墳|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Takarazuka Kofun are located on low hills on the right bank of the Sakanai River, approximately three kilometers to the south of the center of the city of Matsusaka. The site was first [[Excavation (archaeology)|excavated]] in 1928 by a team from [[Mie University]] under the direction of Dr Toshio Sato, and was found to be part of a larger cluster of at least 88 ''kofun'', of which 26 were relatively intact. The site was named the {{nihongo|'''Hanaoka Kofun Group'''|花岡古墳群|}} and was proclaimed a National Historic Site in 1932. However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}

Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.

As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

However, by 1965 encroaching urban development had destroyed 81 of the ''kofun'', and a movement was begun to preserve the remaining seven. However, in the twenty years it took to obtain official injunctions against further destruction and urban development, another three of the remaining ''kofun'' were destroyed. Despite the area official protection being extended in 1978, a road was built directly through the site in the 1980s, destroying another two of the remaining four ''kofun'', and physically separating the remaining pair. The two surviving tumuli were excavated from 1998 to 2003 by the Matsusaka City Board of Education.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4-311-75040-4}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> Since 2005, the tumuli have been part of the {{nihongo|Takarazuka Kofun Park|宝塚古墳公園}}
==Takarazuka Kofun No.1== The Takarazuka Kofun No.1 is a {{nihongo|''[[zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, with a total length of {{convert|111.0|m|ft}}, orientated to the east. It is the largest found in [[Ise Province]] and the 4th largest in Mie Prefecture. It is estimated to have been built in the middle of the Kofun period in the early 5th century AD. Both ''[[fukiishi]]'' and ''[[haniwa]]'' have been found in profusion on the tumulus. The tumulus has a small rectangular stage extending from one side, presumably for ceremonial purposes. When it was re-evcavated by the Mie Prefectural Board of Education from 1999 to 2000, and numerous ''haniwa'' and other funerary objects were uncovered. These, as well as the artifacts uncovered in the 1928 excavations, are preserved at the Matsusaka City Cultural Center. Of especial note is a large boat-shaped ''haniwa'', as well as ''haniwa'' in the shapes of single and multistory houses, and of men in armor. In 2006, these artifacts were collectively designated an [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|National Important Cultural Property]].<ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/00011238 |title=三重県宝塚１号墳出土品|trans-title= Takarazuka Kofun No.1 excavated artifacts|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
The details of the [[burial chamber]], as the interior of the tumulus has not been excavated. [[Ground penetrating radar]] surveys indicate that a chamber with a length of approximately seven meters in a north–south alignment exists about 1.2 meters below the surface of the posterior circular portion; however, as the boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found on the anterior portion of the tumulus, it is most probable that the tumulus contains multiple burial chambers in both sections. The boat-shaped ''haniwa'' was found in a niche created by breaking the ''[[fukiishi]]'' covering of the tumulus, indicating that it was placed at some point after the tumulus had been completed.
The tomb is attributed in local folklore to {{nihongo|Otokazuchi-no-mikoto|乙加豆知命|}}, the deified progenitor of the local Itaka clan.
;Overall length: 111 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 75 meter diameter x 10 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 66 meters wide x 8.1 meters high x 3 tiers
==Takarazuka Kofun No.2== Slightly smaller than The Takarazuka Kofun No.1, this tomb is a {{nihongo|''hotategaigata-kofun''|帆立貝型古墳}}, which is shaped like a [[scallop]] shell when viewed from above. It has an overall length of 90 meters and is orientated to the south-southwest. The posterior circular portion is constructed in three tiers, and the anterior rectangular portion in two-tiers; however, the eastern corner has been partially destroyed due to road construction, despite its official protected status. As with Kofun No.1, ''fukiishi'' and numerous ''haniwa'' have been uncovered. The details of burial chamber are unknown, as the tumulus has not yet been excavated, but from the structure and ''haniwa'', it is estimated to date from the early 5th century AD.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Overall length: 90 meters ;Posterior circular portion: 83 meter diameter x 10.5 meters high x 3 tiers ;Anterior rectangular portion: 40 meters wide x 17 meters long x 2.9 meters high x 2 tiers
Comparing the two tumuli, although the ''hotategaigata-kofun'' is usually considered inferior to the ''[[zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]'' in terms of status, the Takarazuka Kofun No.2 is larger than Takarazuka Kofun No.1, in terms of the diameter and height of the posterior circular portion, despite its shorter length.
==Gallery== <gallery> File:Takarazuka Kofun (Matsusaka)-1, kouenbu.jpg|Takarazuka Kofun No.1 File:Takaraduka-2go-fun 01.JPG|Takarazuka Kofun No.2 File:宝塚1号墳出土 家形埴輪-1.JPG|House-shaped ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical ''haniwa'' File:宝塚1号墳出土 盾形埴輪 (主墳丘出土).JPG|Shield-shaped ''haniwa'' </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Mie)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
